EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into the state Saturday, allowing for an intensification of moisture advection into the area. This was then followed up by a passing cold front and associated cold front, both of which provided lift and helped provide heavy rains across the state. While instability was limited, there was some severe thunderstorms which produced hail during the morning hours of Saturday, March 27th. The main story, however, was the flooding that occurred after the rounds of heavy rain Saturday night into Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer station near the community of Skyline measured 2.65 inches of rainfall in a 24 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain moved across eastern Kentucky from late Friday, February 26th through early Monday, March 1st.  The combination of all the heavy rainfall led to significant flooding across a good portion of central and east Kentucky.  For some areas, this was the most significant flooding in the last 50 to 60 years, or more. FEMA estimates that this event cost $350-400 million dollars in damages, including both individual and public infrastructure. This is now the costliest event in Kentucky to date. ||The first round of heavy rain came through on the morning of Saturday, February 27th.  This early rainfall targeted areas along the Hal Rogers Parkway, including Laurel, Clay, Perry, and Knott counties. Flash flooding let to some closed roads for a short time in these areas.  ||The second round of rainfall occurred late Saturday night as a warm front lifted northward across east Kentucky. The heavy rain stalled out over portions of Powell and Menifee Counties, leading to flash flooding into the early morning hours of Sunday.||Finally, the third round of heavy rain started late Sunday afternoon and continued through Sunday night. Given that much of the area had already received significant amounts of rain from the past two events, the ground was very saturated, and this last round caused widespread flash flooding across much of east Kentucky. As water made it's way to the larger creeks and rivers, these began to flood as well. River levels on the Kentucky river were some of the highest readings ever recorded, namely at Booneville and Ravenna. Clay City, Heidelberg, Jackson, Paintsville, and Salyersville also saw significant river flooding of which hadn't been seen in 40 to 50 years.  ||The periods of heavy rain and flooding led to numerous water rescues and mud/rock slides.  Many areas were cut off or blocked due to the flooding.  Power outages were also noted and reached as high as 12,000 during the peak of the flash flooding Sunday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of 2 to 4 feet of water that is now inundating downtown Martin. Also notified of numerous water rescues ongoing across Floyd County as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog blanketed western Kentucky except the extreme westernmost counties during the late night and early morning hours. Visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile or less. The fog formed under a weak ridge of high pressure that extended from the Great Lakes down across the lower Ohio Valley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report was received of an ongoing water rescue on May Fork Road (Left Fork Otter Creek Road) just before 5am, involving 4 people being rescued from their mobile home as waters rose from nearby Otter Creek. About 30 mins later, 911 dispatch reported that water rescues were still ongoing on May Fork Road and were becoming increasingly difficult as roads were becoming more inundated and flooded from rising waters in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into the state Saturday, allowing for an intensification of moisture advection into the area. This was then followed up by a passing cold front and associated cold front, both of which provided lift and helped provide heavy rains across the state. While instability was limited, there was some severe thunderstorms which produced hail during the morning hours of Saturday, March 27th. The main story, however, was the flooding that occurred after the rounds of heavy rain Saturday night into Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kentucky Mesonet station just north of Whitley City measured 4.05 inches of rainfall in a 24 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog blanketed western Kentucky except the extreme westernmost counties during the late night and early morning hours. Visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile or less. The fog formed under a weak ridge of high pressure that extended from the Great Lakes down across the lower Ohio Valley.

A stalled frontal boundary brought waves of heavy rainfall to central Kentucky from February 26 through February 28. This caused record rainfall, isolated severe winds, and even a tornado. As a result, Bowling Green set a February daily rainfall record with 5.11 on the 28th. The severe winds brought down some trees and a power pole, but the most property damage came from a brief EF1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was flooding on Gray Gap Road at Turner Branch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter/ice storm struck eastern Kentucky from February 14th to 16th 2021. Ice accumulations from freezing rain ranged from 0.25 inches to as much as 0.75 inches by the the time the storm had ended. Sleet accumulations of 0.3 to as much as 2 inches also occurred. The weight of the ice brought down tree limbs, entire trees, power lines, and power poles all across eastern Kentucky. The highest ice accumulations occurred primarily along the higher ridges around eastern Kentucky with much lower amounts in the surrounding valleys. During the height of the icing event, many roads around eastern Kentucky were impassable or nearly so. Many power outages resulted from downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A little over three tenths of an inch of ice accumulation due to freezing rain occurred in and around Inez. The weight of the ice brought down trees and power lines around the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass was centered over the northern Plains, and Kentucky and southern Indiana were positioned directly along a volatile boundary between the arctic air mass and a warm and moist tropical airmass associated with the Gulf of Mexico. The vertical temperature profile was ideal for the formation of freezing rain. The arctic air wedged itself beneath a warm and moist layer aloft. As energy associated with the jet stream passed over the Ohio Valley, a mix of wintry precipitation occurred. A large swath of freezing rain occurred, stretching mainly west to east across Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of 0.25 inches was reported in Stanford, KY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm with heavy mixed precipitation impacted central Kentucky from late on Valentine's Day through the early morning hours of February 16, 2021. The majority of the precipitation occurred on February 15, 2021. A large swath of 1-3 inches of snow fell in addition to heavy sleet totaling 1-3 inches. Freezing rain and rain mixed in as well, especially in southern and eastern Kentucky. Cold air behind the system brought single digit temperatures, with Lexington plummeting to 2 degrees on the morning of February 17, 2021.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain fell across Clark County. The freezing rain and sleet combined to produce a 2-inch thick layer of ice in addition to light snow.

The vertical temperature profile was ideal for the formation of freezing rain. The arctic air wedged itself beneath a warm and moist layer aloft. As energy associated with the jet stream passed over the Ohio Valley, a mix of wintry precipitation occurred. A large swath of freezing rain occurred, stretching mainly west to east across Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleet accumulation of 0.7 inches occurred near Buckner, KY. An additional 0.10 to 0.25 inches of ice accumulation occurred in Buckner and La Grange.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm with heavy mixed precipitation impacted central Kentucky from late on Valentine's Day through the early morning hours of February 16, 2021. The majority of the precipitation occurred on February 15, 2021. A large swath of 1-3 inches of snow fell in addition to heavy sleet totaling 1-3 inches. Freezing rain and rain mixed in as well, especially in southern and eastern Kentucky. Cold air behind the system brought single digit temperatures, with Lexington plummeting to 2 degrees on the morning of February 17, 2021.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy mix of sleet and snow impacted Henry County, KY. 1 to 2 inches of sleet accumulated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall totaling 3-4 inches fell in Hart County, KY with 4 inch totals mainly confined to the southern part of the county.

Freezing rain and rain mixed in as well, especially in southern and eastern Kentucky. Cold air behind the system brought single digit temperatures, with Lexington plummeting to 2 degrees on the morning of February 17, 2021.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy mix of freezing rain and sleet impacted Lincoln County, KY.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant accumulation of freezing rain and sleet impacted Madison County. A quarter inch of ice accumulated on trees and power lines. Sleet ranging from 1.8-4 inches was reported. Downed trees and power lines were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third winter storm in a little more than a week impacted central and eastern Kentucky on February 17-18, 2021. Arctic cold air had pushed far enough south and east by this point that this winter storm featured snow as the dominant precipitation type. Light snow begin during the early evening hours on the 17th. The second wave of this storm exited the region about 24 hours later during the evening of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow of 4-5 inches accumulated in Lincoln County, KY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain moved across eastern Kentucky from late Friday, February 26th through early Monday, March 1st.  The combination of all the heavy rainfall led to significant flooding across a good portion of central and east Kentucky.  For some areas, this was the most significant flooding in the last 50 to 60 years, or more. FEMA estimates that this event cost $350-400 million dollars in damages, including both individual and public infrastructure. This is now the costliest event in Kentucky to date.||The first round of heavy rain came through on the morning of Saturday, February 27th.  This early rainfall targeted areas along the Hal Rogers Parkway, including Laurel, Clay, Perry, and Knott counties. Flash flooding let to some closed roads for a short time in these areas.  ||The second round of rainfall occurred late Saturday night as a warm front lifted northward across east Kentucky. The heavy rain stalled out over portions of Powell and Menifee Counties, leading to flash flooding into the early morning hours of Sunday.||Finally, the third round of heavy rain started late Sunday afternoon and continued through Sunday night. Given that much of the area had already received significant amounts of rain from the past two events, the ground was very saturated, and this last round caused widespread flash flooding across much of east Kentucky. As water made it's way to the larger creeks and rivers, these began to flood as well. River levels on the Kentucky river were some of the highest readings ever recorded, namely at Booneville and Ravenna. Clay City, Heidelberg, Jackson, Paintsville, and Salyersville also saw significant river flooding of which hadn't been seen in 40 to 50 years.  ||The periods of heavy rain and flooding led to numerous water rescues and mud/rock slides.  Many areas were cut off or blocked due to the flooding.  Power outages were also noted and reached as high as 12,000 during the peak of the flash flooding Sunday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of KY Highways 577 and 3630 are covered by high water.

For some areas, this was the most significant flooding in the last 50 to 60 years, or more. FEMA estimates that this event cost $350-400 million dollars in damages, including both individual and public infrastructure. This is now the costliest event in Kentucky to date.||The first round of heavy rain came through on the morning of Saturday, February 27th.  This early rainfall targeted areas along the Hal Rogers Parkway, including Laurel, Clay, Perry, and Knott counties. Flash flooding let to some closed roads for a short time in these areas.  ||The second round of rainfall occurred late Saturday night as a warm front lifted northward across east Kentucky. The heavy rain stalled out over portions of Powell and Menifee Counties, leading to flash flooding into the early morning hours of Sunday.||Finally, the third round of heavy rain started late Sunday afternoon and continued through Sunday night. Given that much of the area had already received significant amounts of rain from the past two events, the ground was very saturated, and this last round caused widespread flash flooding across much of east Kentucky. As water made it's way to the larger creeks and rivers, these began to flood as well. River levels on the Kentucky river were some of the highest readings ever recorded, namely at Booneville and Ravenna. Clay City, Heidelberg, Jackson, Paintsville, and Salyersville also saw significant river flooding of which hadn't been seen in 40 to 50 years.  ||The periods of heavy rain and flooding led to numerous water rescues and mud/rock slides.  Many areas were cut off or blocked due to the flooding.  Power outages were also noted and reached as high as 12,000 during the peak of the flash flooding Sunday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Part of KY-1094 is closed due to high water across the road from the Red River near the Burkhart community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pockets of minor flooding occurred following a heavy rainfall event. Rainfall rates increased the night of the 17th into early on the 18th as a mid level shortwave trough pushed southeastward into the Plains states and interacted with a warm front lifting|northward across the Ohio River Valley. A southwesterly 850 mb low level jet ramped up to 40 to 45 knots, and precipitable water values were very high for this time of year. General rainfall amounts averaged 2 to 3 inches in 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of lesser travelled roads were closed in flood-prone areas. Kentucky 1949 was closed at the tunnel under Interstate 69. Kentucky 1462 was closed at the Clarks River flood plain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening hours in a warm and humid airmass that was in place across the Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very brief and weak tornado touched down near Ribolt, just south of the AA Highway during the evening. Much of the damage was concentrated along the beginning of May Hallow Road. One tree was knocked down and several large limbs were also blown over and placed a few hundred feet from the tree. An outbuilding also sustained damage, which included partial roof collapse. Several eyewitnesses reported a funnel, and video taken near the location of damage shows a funnel extending downward near the ground. Based on an evaluation of the damage, winds associated with this brief tornado were around 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Little River fell below flood stage after near-record flooding on the 28th of February.   The heaviest rain occurred across southern portions of western Kentucky during the morning and afternoon hours on the 28th. This heavy rain was associated with a cold front that trailed from a low pressure system. The low moved from the Plains to the Great Lakes, placing western Kentucky in a very moist warm sector. Hourly rainfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common, leading to storm total rainfall amounts in the 4 to 7 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little River fell back below flood stage after cresting near its record level on the afternoon of the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure center tracked northeast through southeast Missouri into central Illinois during the afternoon and evening. The track of the low resulted in increasing low level winds in western Kentucky in the evening. West to southwest winds gusted to between 40 and 50 mph at times. The peak wind gust in western Kentucky was measured at 48 mph at the mesonet site near Marion.

The track of the low resulted in increasing low level winds in western Kentucky in the evening. West to southwest winds gusted to between 40 and 50 mph at times. The peak wind gust in western Kentucky was measured at 48 mph at the mesonet site near Marion.

An upper level low pressure system moving through the Ohio Valley produced heavy snow along the I-71 corridor. The snow started to fall during the evening of February 8th and continued into the morning hours of February 9th. Six to 10 inches of snow fell across much of the I-71 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of snow fell across the river in Vevay, and it is estimated that 1 to 3 inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved through the Ohio Valley on February 15th to the 16th.  Snow was primarily found northwest and along the I-71 corridor, with more sleet found to the southeast of it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Crittenden reported that snow and sleet accumulated to 4 inches in depth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved through the Ohio Valley on February 15th to the 16th.  Snow was primarily found northwest and along the I-71 corridor, with more sleet found to the southeast of it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on surrounding observations, up to 4 inches of snow and sleet is estimated to have fallen over northern portions of the county. The rest of the county had significant impact to travel due to the sleet that mixed in elsewhere.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms organized into a more consolidated convective system over western Kentucky. The storms organized along and ahead of a cold front moving southeastward into southeast Missouri and adjacent southern Illinois. Cold mid-level temperatures and resulting steep lapse rates supported moderate instability, despite relatively modest low-level moisture. Strong unidirectional deep-layer wind flow resulted in fast storm motion and locally strong wind gusts as the storms crossed the Kentucky Lake region and Pennyrile region of west Kentucky.

A late-season blast of cold air brought sub-freezing temperatures to some areas on the night of the 20th. Lows ranged from 31 to 34 degrees. The arrival of the cold air was heralded by some light snow on the evening of the 19th. Accumulations ranged from a trace to an inch, mainly on grassy and elevated surfaces. At Paducah, this was the latest measurable snowfall on record. The snow had no societal impact. There were no immediate reports of crop damage due to the cold air. A strong cold front moved southeast across the region on the evening of the 19th. A low pressure system then moved northeast along the front, passing over the Tennessee Valley on the 20th. Gusty north winds on the back side of the low ushered in the cold air.

Widespread dense fog blanketed much of western Kentucky except the Hopkinsville area. Visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile or less. The fog formed under a narrow ridge of high pressure that extended up and down the Mississippi River Valley. The fog formed under clear skies and light winds following a day of rain.

Three consecutive nights of frost or freezing temperatures were observed from the 1st to the 3rd. Vegetation was vulnerable to frost and freezing temperatures due to abnormally warm temperatures in March that promoted early growth. The coldest temperatures were on the morning of the 2nd, when lows ranged from 21 to 29 degrees. The exception was around Fulton County, where lows were in the lower 30's. Minor to moderate crop damage was reported after a full assessment was conducted. Alfalfa and wheat sustained light damage in many areas. More than half the peach and apple crop sustained no damage, however a significant percentage of both crops were damaged to varying degrees, ranging from light to severe. Protected strawberries were undamaged, and the overall condition of the crop was rated as good in early May. An unseasonably strong high pressure system with a central pressure around 1035 mb moved southeast from Canada across the lower Ohio Valley on the morning of the 2nd.

Widespread rain fell across the Ohio Valley during the morning hours as a warm front lifted north through the area. Two to three inches of rain fell across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads were closed due to high water throughout the county, including U.S. Route 42 and State Route 467. A mudslide was also reported along U.S. Route 42 E.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance crossed through the Ohio Valley on the evening of April 28th. Ahead of this disturbance, abnormally warm temperatures coupled with high moisture content were in place to aid in shower and thunderstorm develop that evening. In eastern Kentucky a local weather station recorded around 2.8 inches of rain had fallen that evening, which ultimately led to a few instances of flash flooding between the town of Grayson and Rush, especially along Route 60. A local water rescue was conducted after a vehicle became swept up by the quickly rising water from Wilson Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 60 became flooded between Grayson and Rush due to heavy rainfall. A water rescue was conducted along Wilson Creek after a vehicle became submerged by the quickly rising water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moving through the Ohio Valley produced heavy snow along the I-71 corridor. The snow started to fall during the evening of February 8th and continued into the morning hours of February 9th. Six to 10 inches of snow fell across much of the I-71 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 12:30 am on the 9th, a public report from Elsmere indicated that 6.5 inches of snow had fallen. By daybreak, a NWS employee in Fort Mitchell measured 8 inches of accumulation, as did a social media report from Erlanger.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three consecutive nights of frost or freezing temperatures were observed from the 1st to the 3rd. Vegetation was vulnerable to frost and freezing temperatures due to abnormally warm temperatures in March that promoted early growth. The coldest temperatures were on the morning of the 2nd, when lows ranged from 21 to 29 degrees. The exception was around Fulton County, where lows were in the lower 30's. Minor to moderate crop damage was reported after a full assessment was conducted. Alfalfa and wheat sustained light damage in many areas. More than half the peach and apple crop sustained no damage, however a significant percentage of both crops were damaged to varying degrees, ranging from light to severe. Protected strawberries were undamaged, and the overall condition of the crop was rated as good in early May. An unseasonably strong high pressure system with a central pressure around 1035 mb moved southeast from Canada across the lower Ohio Valley on the morning of the 2nd.

A warm front lifted north into the state Saturday, allowing for an intensification of moisture advection into the area. This was then followed up by a passing cold front and associated cold front, both of which provided lift and helped provide heavy rains across the state. While instability was limited, there was some severe thunderstorms which produced hail during the morning hours of Saturday, March 27th. The main story, however, was the flooding that occurred after the rounds of heavy rain Saturday night into Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hwy 72 near Caball Estates in the Catrons Creek community is impassable according to pictures posted to social media.

The third significant winter storm in a week moved into the area during the late evening hours of February 17th. What began as snow, transitioned to a wintry mix in some areas as the night progressed. The highest snow totals were generally in northeastern Kentucky through northern West Virginia. The local public and cooperative observers noted between 3 to 5 inches of snow fell across northeastern Kentucky.||Two unattended elderly woman died in their homes from hypothermia in Boyd County on the afternoon during this storm. Their power was out due to a previously significant ice storm that prompted three quarters of the county to lose power due to downed trees and powerlines. With hazardous weather ongoing, power companies struggled to restore power across eastern Kentucky in time for this event. The snow accumulating on top of ice from previous storms also caused additional tree damage and power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yards were reported flooded and water was surrounding some homes in the Elcomb community of Harlan County.

This was then followed up by a passing cold front and associated cold front, both of which provided lift and helped provide heavy rains across the state. While instability was limited, there was some severe thunderstorms which produced hail during the morning hours of Saturday, March 27th. The main story, however, was the flooding that occurred after the rounds of heavy rain Saturday night into Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Officials were in the process of closing one lane of Hwy 119 due to high water over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moving through the Ohio Valley produced heavy snow along the I-71 corridor. The snow started to fall during the evening of February 8th and continued into the morning hours of February 9th. Six to 10 inches of snow fell across much of the I-71 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of snow fell in Crittenden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three consecutive nights of frost or freezing temperatures were observed from the 1st to the 3rd. Vegetation was vulnerable to frost and freezing temperatures due to abnormally warm temperatures in March that promoted early growth. The coldest temperatures were on the morning of the 2nd, when lows ranged from 21 to 29 degrees. The exception was around Fulton County, where lows were in the lower 30's. Minor to moderate crop damage was reported after a full assessment was conducted. Alfalfa and wheat sustained light damage in many areas. More than half the peach and apple crop sustained no damage, however a significant percentage of both crops were damaged to varying degrees, ranging from light to severe. Protected strawberries were undamaged, and the overall condition of the crop was rated as good in early May. An unseasonably strong high pressure system with a central pressure around 1035 mb moved southeast from Canada across the lower Ohio Valley on the morning of the 2nd.

Strong gusty west to southwest winds occurred in the wake of a cold front that arced southeast from a low pressure center over central Illinois. Peak measured wind gusts were near 40 mph along and south of a line from Paducah to Madisonville. The highest measured gusts were 40 mph at Paducah and 41 mph at Fort Campbell/Hopkinsville.

The third significant winter storm in a week moved into the area during the late evening hours of February 17th. What began as snow, transitioned to a wintry mix in some areas as the night progressed. The highest snow totals were generally in northeastern Kentucky through northern West Virginia. The local public and cooperative observers noted between 3 to 5 inches of snow fell across northeastern Kentucky.||Two unattended elderly woman died in their homes from hypothermia in Boyd County on the afternoon during this storm. Their power was out due to a previously significant ice storm that prompted three quarters of the county to lose power due to downed trees and powerlines. With hazardous weather ongoing, power companies struggled to restore power across eastern Kentucky in time for this event. The snow accumulating on top of ice from previous storms also caused additional tree damage and power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter south of Ribolt indicated that up to a half inch of a glaze was found on surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of wintry precipitation affected the region, with heavier ice accumulation found across northern and northeast Kentucky and far southern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer in Highland Heights measured 3 inches of snow while another east of Wilder measured 2.7 inches and one south of Covington measured 2.3 inches. A public report from Independence showed that 2 inches fell there.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of wintry precipitation affected the region, with heavier ice accumulation found across northern and northeast Kentucky and far southern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer located 5 miles northwest of California measured 2.6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of wintry precipitation affected the region, with heavier ice accumulation found across northern and northeast Kentucky and far southern Ohio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of storms, followed by a bowing line of thunderstorms, organized and accelerated east-northeastward during the overnight and wee morning hours. Several tornadoes accompanied mesovortices along the bowing line of storms. In addition, a particularly strong microburst was associated with the line. Finally, the occurrence of multiple thunderstorms resulted in localized flash flooding in the Pennyrile region. A mid-level shortwave trough moved east across the southern Plains. A belt of stronger southwesterly 500 mb winds spread northeast ahead of the trough into southeast Missouri and Arkansas. Instability was sufficient for a round of severe  thunderstorms to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were snapped. Many tree branches and some trees fell on multiple homes, which inflicted damage. At one point there were 65 trees covering main and secondary roads across the county. All main roads were back open on the afternoon of the 4th, and crews then focused on secondary roads. Many roads were closed as cleanup continued. Emergency management reported 28 residential structures had some form of damage, and a dozen commercial structures were damaged as well. These included farming structures and equipment. One family was displaced from their mobile home because of damage from a fallen tree. A county state of emergency was declared. Peak winds were estimated near 105 mph.

A wintry mix of precipitation impacted the region resulting in icy roads, numerous accidents, road closures, and scattered power outages. About 3,000 customers in the Owensboro area were without power. The event started in the early morning hours of February 10th and continued into February 11th.  Icy roads on the morning of February 10th resulted in one vehicle fatality (indirect fatality) in Trigg County on Highway 80 at the Trace Highway in Land-Between-The-Lakes National Recreation Area. Numerous accidents occurred from the morning hours of the 10th into the 11th. This resulted in lengthy road closures on the Audubon Parkway west of Owensboro, as well as sections of I-24 between Calvert City and Hopkinsville.  Ice accumulations ranging from 0.1 to 0.25 were commonplace across western Kentucky. A narrow zone of higher ice amounts, ranging from 0.25 to 0.5, occurred from Shawneetown, IL eastward into the Henderson and Owensboro areas. Scattered power outages and downed tree limbs were primarily focused in this zone of higher accumulations as well. Most areas received at least minor accumulations of sleet along the Ohio River, but the heaviest swath of sleet amounts ranging from 0.5 to 1.5 occurred north of the Ohio River. The winter storm was the result of a moist southwest 500 mb flow and an embedded low-amplitude impulse. An inverted surface trough developing in the general vicinity of Arkansas brought another swath of precipitation. As the inverted trough pulled away to the east, colder air was drawn southward and changed the freezing rain to sleet.

A stationary frontal boundary remained just north of the state on the 11th and first part of the day 12th, placing much of southeast Kentucky in the warm sector, and building instability. As this cold front quickly pushed southward during the afternoon hours on June the 12th, scattered storms developed. These storms became strong to severe as they moved into this unstable and untapped warm/moist airmass, leading to widespread wind damage across portions of the Jackson County Warning Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down northeast of the Meece Community.

A high impact event affected eastern Kentucky beginning on the morning of February 10th, as a wave of precipitation pressed into the area in response to a stationary boundary close by. This resulted in a mixed bag of winter precipitation across the area, with significant ice accumulations in eastern Kentucky. A quarter to almost half an inch of ice accumulations were measured by local cooperative observers in addition to an inch of snow that fell during this event.||Significant damage from this storm included extensive tree damage, widespread power outages, and terrible road conditions in Greenup and Carter Counties. Icicles developed on power lines and cables, which prompted further problems for future February winter events.

Numerous accidents occurred from the morning hours of the 10th into the 11th. This resulted in lengthy road closures on the Audubon Parkway west of Owensboro, as well as sections of I-24 between Calvert City and Hopkinsville.  Ice accumulations ranging from 0.1 to 0.25 were commonplace across western Kentucky. A narrow zone of higher ice amounts, ranging from 0.25 to 0.5, occurred from Shawneetown, IL eastward into the Henderson and Owensboro areas. Scattered power outages and downed tree limbs were primarily focused in this zone of higher accumulations as well. Most areas received at least minor accumulations of sleet along the Ohio River, but the heaviest swath of sleet amounts ranging from 0.5 to 1.5 occurred north of the Ohio River. The winter storm was the result of a moist southwest 500 mb flow and an embedded low-amplitude impulse. An inverted surface trough developing in the general vicinity of Arkansas brought another swath of precipitation. As the inverted trough pulled away to the east, colder air was drawn southward and changed the freezing rain to sleet.

Waves of rain, heavy at times, moved across the region during the afternoon of February 26th through the morning of March 1st. Significant flooding began on the last day of February in Lawrence County, where water flowing along Kentucky Route 3 began to reach adjacent homes as well as the Fallsburg campground was completely submerged underwater.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding was observed along Kentucky Route 3 with water beginning to flow into adjacent homes. The Fallsburg campground was almost completely underwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of storms, followed by a bowing line of thunderstorms, organized and accelerated east-northeastward during the overnight and wee morning hours. Several tornadoes accompanied mesovortices along the bowing line of storms. In addition, a particularly strong microburst was associated with the line. Finally, the occurrence of multiple thunderstorms resulted in localized flash flooding in the Pennyrile region. A mid-level shortwave trough moved east across the southern Plains. A belt of stronger southwesterly 500 mb winds spread northeast ahead of the trough into southeast Missouri and Arkansas. Instability was sufficient for a round of severe  thunderstorms to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The narrow, well-defined path was in a rural farmland and wooded area. A well-constructed modern home suffered minor to moderate roof damage. The tornado followed a creek bed for the last quarter mile, where a small patch of very large trees was snapped or uprooted. A well head was blown into a house, damaging the siding. Peak winds were estimated near 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary frontal boundary remained just north of the state on the 11th and first part of the day 12th, placing much of southeast Kentucky in the warm sector, and building instability. As this cold front quickly pushed southward during the afternoon hours on June the 12th, scattered storms developed. These storms became strong to severe as they moved into this unstable and untapped warm/moist airmass, leading to widespread wind damage across portions of the Jackson County Warning Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down along Sinking Creek Road near the Hal Rogers Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary brought waves of heavy rainfall to central Kentucky from February 26 through February 28. This caused record rainfall, isolated severe winds, and even a tornado. As a result, Bowling Green set a February daily rainfall record with 5.11 on the 28th. The severe winds brought down some trees and a power pole, but the most property damage came from a brief EF1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several structures were damaged along Highway 558.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary corridor of thunderstorms developed from central Kentucky to northwest Tennessee, including Christian and Todd Counties. These storms resulted in excessive rainfall amounts over places that received heavy rain during the early morning severe storms. The storms occurred in a narrow axis of instability along a southeastward moving cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicles were stalled out in downtown Hopkinsville, as shown in a photo posted on social media.

The porch was torn off one of the mobile homes. A rainfall measurement of 5.01 inches was received from a weather station six miles southwest of Greenville.

Clusters of thunderstorms organized into a more consolidated convective system over western Kentucky. The storms organized along and ahead of a cold front moving southeastward into southeast Missouri and adjacent southern Illinois. Cold mid-level temperatures and resulting steep lapse rates supported moderate instability, despite relatively modest low-level moisture. Strong unidirectional deep-layer wind flow resulted in fast storm motion and locally strong wind gusts as the storms crossed the Kentucky Lake region and Pennyrile region of west Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree landed on a mobile home. A tree landed on a power line in northern Murray. Forty customers were without power in Murray.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cluster of severe storms developed over southeast Indiana and Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours. After sunset, these storms became more linear and quickly advanced southward with a strong outflow gust front. This gust front and trailing line of storms produced a swath of minor wind damage as they plowed into northeast Kentucky during the mid-to-late evening hours but rapidly weakened after dropping south of the Mountain Parkway. No weather observing stations in the Jackson forecast area measured severe wind gusts; however, a 67 mph gust was recorded at the Fleming Mason Airport just north of the Fleming County line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rowan County dispatch reported that a strong thunderstorm gust front caused multiple trees to fall onto Laurel Road near Elliotville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cluster of severe storms developed over southeast Indiana and Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours. After sunset, these storms became more linear and quickly advanced southward with a strong outflow gust front. This gust front and trailing line of storms produced a swath of minor wind damage as they plowed into northeast Kentucky during the mid-to-late evening hours but rapidly weakened after dropping south of the Mountain Parkway. No weather observing stations in the Jackson forecast area measured severe wind gusts; however, a 67 mph gust was recorded at the Fleming Mason Airport just north of the Fleming County line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee saw a large tree limb that had been broken by the thunderstorm gust front and subsequently fell onto KY-30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving, yet significant winter storm moved through the area on the morning of February 15th. As the system moved through, a warm nose of air moving northward resulted in a second round of mixed precipitation in just a few short days after the previous storm. ||The ice accumulations from the system provided the biggest impacts across the region. This was the second significant ice event in 5 days. As a result, once again tree damage and power outages occurred. According to the local utility company, around 99 percent of Lawrence County and 60 percent of Boyd County were without power due to downed trees and powerlines.

A wintry mix of precipitation impacted the region resulting in icy roads, numerous accidents, road closures, and scattered power outages. About 3,000 customers in the Owensboro area were without power. The event started in the early morning hours of February 10th and continued into February 11th.  Icy roads on the morning of February 10th resulted in one vehicle fatality (indirect fatality) in Trigg County on Highway 80 at the Trace Highway in Land-Between-The-Lakes National Recreation Area. Numerous accidents occurred from the morning hours of the 10th into the 11th. This resulted in lengthy road closures on the Audubon Parkway west of Owensboro, as well as sections of I-24 between Calvert City and Hopkinsville.  Ice accumulations ranging from 0.1 to 0.25 were commonplace across western Kentucky. A narrow zone of higher ice amounts, ranging from 0.25 to 0.5, occurred from Shawneetown, IL eastward into the Henderson and Owensboro areas. Scattered power outages and downed tree limbs were primarily focused in this zone of higher accumulations as well. Most areas received at least minor accumulations of sleet along the Ohio River, but the heaviest swath of sleet amounts ranging from 0.5 to 1.5 occurred north of the Ohio River. The winter storm was the result of a moist southwest 500 mb flow and an embedded low-amplitude impulse. An inverted surface trough developing in the general vicinity of Arkansas brought another swath of precipitation. As the inverted trough pulled away to the east, colder air was drawn southward and changed the freezing rain to sleet.

A nearly stationary corridor of thunderstorms developed from central Kentucky to northwest Tennessee, including Christian and Todd Counties. These storms resulted in excessive rainfall amounts over places that received heavy rain during the early morning severe storms. The storms occurred in a narrow axis of instability along a southeastward moving cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little River went above flood stage in response to two rounds of heavy rain. The moderate river flooding caused widespread inundation of low-lying fields and cropland. There was considerable flooding of a few streets in Hopkinsville. At the Hopkinsville river gage at the by-pass, the crest was around 1.5 feet lower than the major flood of July, 2016. At the river gage at U.S. Highway 68 east of the city, the crest was about two feet lower than the major July, 2016 flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary frontal boundary remained just north of the state on the 11th and first part of the day 12th, placing much of southeast Kentucky in the warm sector, and building instability. As this cold front quickly pushed southward during the afternoon hours on June the 12th, scattered storms developed. These storms became strong to severe as they moved into this unstable and untapped warm/moist airmass, leading to widespread wind damage across portions of the Jackson County Warning Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down just east of the Ferguson Community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center was located over southern Indiana, with a cold front extending southward through west-central Kentucky during the early afternoon hours on Thursday, June 3rd. This system would continue to push eastward throughout the afternoon, before the cold front traversed eastern Kentucky during the overnight hours heading into June 4th. Showers and thunderstorms began to develop during the afternoon hours on June 3rd, ahead of the approaching front, amid deep moisture advection and an influx of warm and unstable air. Storms that moved through the south-east portion of the state resulted in some damaging wind gusts, namely in McCreary and Whitley Counties. Meanwhile, enough rotation was in place that a funnel cloud was reported in Knox County as a storm went through there. Luckily there were no reports or evidence that this funnel cloud touched down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down along Hwy-1045 near the community of Beulah Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a cool morning with some showers, clouds began to break and allow weak to moderate instability to build ahead of a weak prefrontal surface trough, located over central Kentucky and Middle Tennessee. Across eastern Kentucky, the most favorable instability and shear environment was observed southwest of KY-15. By mid-afternoon, storms were firing over the Lake Cumberland area and tracking from southwest-to-northeast onto fairly remote portions of the Cumberland Plateau. During the early and mid-evening, the focus for the strongest storms shifted eastward toward Pine and Black mountains before waning with the loss of daytime heating and the passage of a trailing cold front. Many of these storms featured rotating updrafts which produced swaths of small to marginally severe hail. The strongest storms produced up to half dollar size hail in Bell and Clay counties. In Bell County, some roadways were nearly covered by hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An individual shared a video on social media of up to nickel-sized hail falling near Calvin, KY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of storms, followed by a bowing line of thunderstorms, organized and accelerated east-northeastward during the overnight and wee morning hours. Several tornadoes accompanied mesovortices along the bowing line of storms. In addition, a particularly strong microburst was associated with the line. Finally, the occurrence of multiple thunderstorms resulted in localized flash flooding in the Pennyrile region. A mid-level shortwave trough moved east across the southern Plains. A belt of stronger southwesterly 500 mb winds spread northeast ahead of the trough into southeast Missouri and Arkansas. Instability was sufficient for a round of severe  thunderstorms to occur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unexpected heavy snow event occurred across Bell, Harlan, and Letcher counties on February 7th, 2021. Snowfall amounts varied from an inch in Thornton in Letcher county to nearly 10 inches  near Rock House in Harlan county. Several other locations had snowfall amounts ranging from 3 to nearly 7 inches. Nearly 4000 power outages were reported across our forecast area as a result of this snow event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported that 4.5 inches of snow had fallen in Strunk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter/ice storm struck eastern Kentucky from February 14th to 16th 2021. Ice accumulations from freezing rain ranged from 0.25 inches to as much as 0.75 inches by the the time the storm had ended. Sleet accumulations of 0.3 to as much as 2 inches also occurred. The weight of the ice brought down tree limbs, entire trees, power lines, and power poles all across eastern Kentucky. The highest ice accumulations occurred primarily along the higher ridges around eastern Kentucky with much lower amounts in the surrounding valleys. During the height of the icing event, many roads around eastern Kentucky were impassable or nearly so. Many power outages resulted from downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was brought down by the weight of ice near Mashfork.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist northerly flow and cold temperatures lead to ideal conditions for snow across eastern Kentucky to begin the month of February 2021. On average, 1-3 inches of snow fell across eastern Kentucky from late Sunday night through early Tuesday morning. Slick, snow covered roads were the primary hazard during and immediately following this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A volunteer weather observer reported that 1.7 inches of snow had fallen near Lerose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into the state Saturday, allowing for an intensification of moisture advection into the area. This was then followed up by a passing cold front and associated cold front, both of which provided lift and helped provide heavy rains across the state. While instability was limited, there was some severe thunderstorms which produced hail during the morning hours of Saturday, March 27th. The main story, however, was the flooding that occurred after the rounds of heavy rain Saturday night into Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter in the city of Corbin measured 3.15 inches of rainfall in a 24 hour period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of snow was reported in Hyden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was pushing across eastern KY during the afternoon and evening hours on June 13th. Ahead of this front, a warm and moist airmass provided the perfect environment for thunderstorms to develop. These thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts and small hail, with numerous reports of trees being blown down as they rolled through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported as being blown down near the Greenmount Community.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported being blown down in this location near the Pittsburg Community, taking power lines down with them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of storms, followed by a bowing line of thunderstorms, organized and accelerated east-northeastward during the overnight and wee morning hours. Several tornadoes accompanied mesovortices along the bowing line of storms. In addition, a particularly strong microburst was associated with the line. Finally, the occurrence of multiple thunderstorms resulted in localized flash flooding in the Pennyrile region. A mid-level shortwave trough moved east across the southern Plains. A belt of stronger southwesterly 500 mb winds spread northeast ahead of the trough into southeast Missouri and Arkansas. Instability was sufficient for a round of severe  thunderstorms to occur.

A nearly stationary corridor of thunderstorms developed from central Kentucky to northwest Tennessee, including Christian and Todd Counties. These storms resulted in excessive rainfall amounts over places that received heavy rain during the early morning severe storms. The storms occurred in a narrow axis of instability along a southeastward moving cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the Green River, following two rounds of thunderstorms on the 4th. At the Paradise river gage, the river crested at 387.4 feet on the night of the 7th. Flood stage is 380 feet. There was considerable flooding of low-lying fields and woodlands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary corridor of thunderstorms developed from central Kentucky to northwest Tennessee, including Christian and Todd Counties. These storms resulted in excessive rainfall amounts over places that received heavy rain during the early morning severe storms. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Green River following the heavy rain on the 4th. Some low-lying woods and fields near the river were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary frontal boundary remained just north of the state on the 11th and first part of the day 12th, placing much of southeast Kentucky in the warm sector, and building instability. As this cold front quickly pushed southward during the afternoon hours on June the 12th, scattered storms developed. These storms became strong to severe as they moved into this unstable and untapped warm/moist airmass, leading to widespread wind damage across portions of the Jackson County Warning Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down near the Woodstock Community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist northerly flow and cold temperatures lead to ideal conditions for snow across eastern Kentucky to begin the month of February 2021. On average, 1-3 inches of snow fell across eastern Kentucky from late Sunday night through early Tuesday morning. Slick, snow covered roads were the primary hazard during and immediately following this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported that 2.5 inches of snow had fallen in Rowdy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter/ice storm struck eastern Kentucky from February 14th to 16th 2021. Ice accumulations from freezing rain ranged from 0.25 inches to as much as 0.75 inches by the the time the storm had ended. Sleet accumulations of 0.3 to as much as 2 inches also occurred. The weight of the ice brought down tree limbs, entire trees, power lines, and power poles all across eastern Kentucky. The highest ice accumulations occurred primarily along the higher ridges around eastern Kentucky with much lower amounts in the surrounding valleys. During the height of the icing event, many roads around eastern Kentucky were impassable or nearly so. Many power outages resulted from downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around a third of an inch of ice accumulation due to freezing rain occurred at elevations above 1300 feet in Knott county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the midst of a widespread heavy rainfall event, a short-lived tornado produced damage in portions of Clinton and Wayne Counties in southern Kentucky. ||Thunderstorms were embedded in the heavier rainfall throughout the day and evening. A line of storms developed across south-central Kentucky, and began to bow out as it moved eastward across Clinton County. The bow then showed signs of breaking, which results in heightened rotational potential. Damage was then reported in both Clinton and Wayne Counties by local Emergency Management. This prompted the Louisville office to conduct a storm survey. ||From this survey, it was concluded that a short-lived EF1 tornado tracked across portions of Clinton County (Louisville NWS County Warning Area) and then briefly into Wayne County, before dissipating. Debris from Clinton County was also carried over into Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado narrowed as it crossed from Clinton County (Louisville, KY County Warning Area) into Wayne County, with some straight line wind damage noted. The winds|generally were from the west to southwest surrounding the tornado.|The straight line winds of 65 to 75 mph damaged 2 barns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossed through the region on March 18th accompanied by a cold front at the surface. Peaks of sunshine during the late morning and early afternoon allowed the atmosphere to become unstable and develop severe thunderstorms as the frontal boundary trekked through the state. Gusty winds within strong storms led to a small tree being blown down in Carter County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tree was blown down along with several large limbs along New Castle Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on February 28th sent the Ohio and Green Rivers above flood stage early in March. The flooding was moderate in some places, especially on the Green River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred along the Green River. The river crested at 29.69 feet on the Calhoun river gage. This was well above flood stage of 23 feet. This was the highest crest since March 1, 2018, when the river crested at 31.67 feet. Portions of Calhoun and Rumsey were flooded, but few if any residences were evacuated. Thousands of acres were flooded in the Island, Rumsey, and Jewel City areas, but the cropland was mostly dormant. Floodwaters approached Kentucky Highway 81 near the Green River bridge. Some county roads were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on February 28th sent the Ohio and Green Rivers above flood stage early in March. The flooding was moderate in some places, especially on the Green River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Ohio River. However, the combination of elevated river levels on the Green River flowing into the swollen Ohio River produced extensive backwater flooding across Daviess County. Panther Creek was among some of the tributaries that experienced backwater flooding. Many low-lying fields and woodlands were flooded. Over a dozen roads were closed, mostly lesser travelled county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on February 28th sent the Ohio and Green Rivers above flood stage early in March. The flooding was moderate in some places, especially on the Green River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding affected low-lying woods and fields. Some secondary roads in the bottomlands were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on February 28th sent the Ohio and Green Rivers above flood stage early in March. The flooding was moderate in some places, especially on the Green River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Ohio River. Low-lying bottomland fields and woodlands were flooded. A campground at Birdsville was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on February 28th sent the Ohio and Green Rivers above flood stage early in March. The flooding was moderate in some places, especially on the Green River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Ohio River. Low-lying woods and farmland near the river were flooded. Some lesser-travelled river access roads were flooded. Public parks along the Paducah riverfront were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of rain changed over to snow during the evening of the 20th. As the system moved east, it ultimately produced 1 to 3 inches of snow. Given the warm nature of the ground, this overnight snow did not produce traditional travel issues. Some trees that had leafed out were knocked down by the snow and did create some obstacles on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter southwest of Union City measured 3 inches of snow. The CVG airport measured 2.7 inches of snow. A CoCoRaHS observer southwest of Burlington measured 2.5 inches, while a public report from Burlington measured 2.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a stationary boundary parked just north of the state, and deep S to SW flow, much of the state was seeing ample amounts of warm air and moisture being advected in. Afternoon storms developed each day, each capable of producing torrential rainfall. While the impacts were somewhat isolated on the 9th and 10th (Wednesday and Thursday), more scattered to widespread convection was forecast for Friday the 11th, leading to a Flash Flood Watch being issued through the day. Multiple flash flood reports were received this day, with the hardest hit area being Beattyville and Lee County, where considerable flash flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station in Rowan County measured 1.58 inches of rainfall in a 24-hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe storm produced strong winds just west of Hopkinsville. A surface low pressure center tracked northeast through southeast Missouri into central Illinois during the afternoon and evening. The track of the low resulted in increasing low level moisture and low level wind shear in western Kentucky in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two separate spotters measured a wind gust to 61 mph on Highway 68/80 near the Trigg County line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding along the Ohio and Mississippi Rivers affected portions of the county, mainly sparsely populated bottomland in the flood plain. Some rural county roads were closed. A wildlife management area in the river floodplain was mostly underwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a cool morning with some showers, clouds began to break and allow weak to moderate instability to build ahead of a weak prefrontal surface trough, located over central Kentucky and Middle Tennessee. Across eastern Kentucky, the most favorable instability and shear environment was observed southwest of KY-15. By mid-afternoon, storms were firing over the Lake Cumberland area and tracking from southwest-to-northeast onto fairly remote portions of the Cumberland Plateau. During the early and mid-evening, the focus for the strongest storms shifted eastward toward Pine and Black mountains before waning with the loss of daytime heating and the passage of a trailing cold front. Many of these storms featured rotating updrafts which produced swaths of small to marginally severe hail. The strongest storms produced up to half dollar size hail in Bell and Clay counties. In Bell County, some roadways were nearly covered by hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An individual shared a picture of up to approximately quarter-sized hail on social media.

The weight of the ice brought down tree limbs, entire trees, power lines, and power poles all across eastern Kentucky. The highest ice accumulations occurred primarily along the higher ridges around eastern Kentucky with much lower amounts in the surrounding valleys. During the height of the icing event, many roads around eastern Kentucky were impassable or nearly so. Many power outages resulted from downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half an inch of sleet accumulation was observed near Jeffersonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a cool morning with some showers, clouds began to break and allow weak to moderate instability to build ahead of a weak prefrontal surface trough, located over central Kentucky and Middle Tennessee. Across eastern Kentucky, the most favorable instability and shear environment was observed southwest of KY-15. By mid-afternoon, storms were firing over the Lake Cumberland area and tracking from southwest-to-northeast onto fairly remote portions of the Cumberland Plateau. During the early and mid-evening, the focus for the strongest storms shifted eastward toward Pine and Black mountains before waning with the loss of daytime heating and the passage of a trailing cold front. Many of these storms featured rotating updrafts which produced swaths of small to marginally severe hail. The strongest storms produced up to half dollar size hail in Bell and Clay counties. In Bell County, some roadways were nearly covered by hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported up to nickel-sized hail along Langley Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on February 28th sent the Ohio and Green Rivers above flood stage early in March. The flooding was moderate in some places, especially on the Green River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred along the Green River. The river crested at 393.73 feet at Paradise, which was the highest crest since May of 2011. There were only five higher crests on record, including the floods of May, 2010 and May, 2011. Flood stage is 380 feet. There was extensive flooding of low-lying fields and farmland, in addition to some rural roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into the state Saturday, allowing for an intensification of moisture advection into the area. This was then followed up by a passing cold front and associated cold front, both of which provided lift and helped provide heavy rains across the state. While instability was limited, there was some severe thunderstorms which produced hail during the morning hours of Saturday, March 27th. The main story, however, was the flooding that occurred after the rounds of heavy rain Saturday night into Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter in Mount Vernon reported 2.90 inches of rainfall in a 24 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to eastern Kentucky on February 17th and 18th 2021. Storm total snowfall amounts varied from as little as an inch to as much as 6 inches in a couple of locations. Many locations around the area received 3.5 to 4 inches of snow from this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of snow was reported near Bulan.

Snowfall amounts varied from an inch in Thornton in Letcher county to nearly 10 inches  near Rock House in Harlan county. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A resident of Beverly reported that 2.5 inches of snow had fallen at his location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a stationary boundary parked just north of the state, and deep S to SW flow, much of the state was seeing ample amounts of warm air and moisture being advected in. While the impacts were somewhat isolated on the 9th and 10th (Wednesday and Thursday), more scattered to widespread convection was forecast for Friday the 11th, leading to a Flash Flood Watch being issued through the day. Multiple flash flood reports were received this day, with the hardest hit area being Beattyville and Lee County, where considerable flash flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer in Oneida measured 1.93 inches of rainfall in a 24-hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a cool morning with some showers, clouds began to break and allow weak to moderate instability to build ahead of a weak prefrontal surface trough, located over central Kentucky and Middle Tennessee. Across eastern Kentucky, the most favorable instability and shear environment was observed southwest of KY-15. By mid-afternoon, storms were firing over the Lake Cumberland area and tracking from southwest-to-northeast onto fairly remote portions of the Cumberland Plateau. During the early and mid-evening, the focus for the strongest storms shifted eastward toward Pine and Black mountains before waning with the loss of daytime heating and the passage of a trailing cold front. Many of these storms featured rotating updrafts which produced swaths of small to marginally severe hail. The strongest storms produced up to half dollar size hail in Bell and Clay counties. In Bell County, some roadways were nearly covered by hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An employee at the Family Dollar in Livingston reported that up to quarter-sized hail fell near the store.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and occluded low pressure system moved through the state on Thursday, March the 18th. Deep moisture and steep lapse rates set the stage for convection during the afternoon. With freezing levels being very low, these storms were able to create copious amounts of hail in addition to torrential rainfall and excessive lightning. While only a few storms reached severe hail potential, a couple dozen others produced widespread small hail. Meanwhile, many of these storms began to train across the same locations, bringing multiple rounds of torrential rainfall. This then resulted in flash flooding during the late afternoon and evening hours. Light to moderate rainfall continued into the overnight hours, as wrap around moisture from the passing low took hold of eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that 2 to 3 feet of water was flowing across KY 1427 near the intersection of Abbott Creek Road and Workmans Branch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter/ice storm struck eastern Kentucky from February 14th to 16th 2021. Ice accumulations from freezing rain ranged from 0.25 inches to as much as 0.75 inches by the the time the storm had ended. Sleet accumulations of 0.3 to as much as 2 inches also occurred. The weight of the ice brought down tree limbs, entire trees, power lines, and power poles all across eastern Kentucky. The highest ice accumulations occurred primarily along the higher ridges around eastern Kentucky with much lower amounts in the surrounding valleys. During the height of the icing event, many roads around eastern Kentucky were impassable or nearly so. Many power outages resulted from downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half an inch of ice accumulation due to freezing rain occurred in and around West Liberty.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a cool morning with some showers, clouds began to break and allow weak to moderate instability to build ahead of a weak prefrontal surface trough, located over central Kentucky and Middle Tennessee. Across eastern Kentucky, the most favorable instability and shear environment was observed southwest of KY-15. By mid-afternoon, storms were firing over the Lake Cumberland area and tracking from southwest-to-northeast onto fairly remote portions of the Cumberland Plateau. During the early and mid-evening, the focus for the strongest storms shifted eastward toward Pine and Black mountains before waning with the loss of daytime heating and the passage of a trailing cold front. Many of these storms featured rotating updrafts which produced swaths of small to marginally severe hail. The strongest storms produced up to half dollar size hail in Bell and Clay counties. In Bell County, some roadways were nearly covered by hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An individual shared pictures of approximately nickel-sized hail that fell at their residence on Shadow Wolf Hill Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and occluded low pressure system moved through the state on Thursday, March the 18th. Deep moisture and steep lapse rates set the stage for convection during the afternoon. With freezing levels being very low, these storms were able to create copious amounts of hail in addition to torrential rainfall and excessive lightning. While only a few storms reached severe hail potential, a couple dozen others produced widespread small hail. Meanwhile, many of these storms began to train across the same locations, bringing multiple rounds of torrential rainfall. This then resulted in flash flooding during the late afternoon and evening hours. Light to moderate rainfall continued into the overnight hours, as wrap around moisture from the passing low took hold of eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported in the community of Hatfield, ranging in size from dimes to quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a stationary boundary parked just north of the state, and deep S to SW flow, much of the state was seeing ample amounts of warm air and moisture being advected in. Afternoon storms developed each day, each capable of producing torrential rainfall. While the impacts were somewhat isolated on the 9th and 10th (Wednesday and Thursday), more scattered to widespread convection was forecast for Friday the 11th, leading to a Flash Flood Watch being issued through the day. Multiple flash flood reports were received this day, with the hardest hit area being Beattyville and Lee County, where considerable flash flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public measured 2.30 inches of rainfall in a 24-hour period just S of the London-Corbin Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted northward over the region. Cold air at the ground was slow to respond and some light ice accumulations were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The instrumentation at the Cincinnati Airport KCVG indicated that 0.07 inches of ice accrual had occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the daytime hours on January 25th, a strong low pressure system began approaching Kentucky from the west, resulting in a warm front lifting towards the region from the south. A surface trough was also analyzed across the state during the day. As this system neared throughout the day, elevated instability increased, as did the upper level wind speeds. Thunder was reported with rain showers on the morning of the 25th, but convective activity increased heading into the evening as the warm front lifted north into the state, increasing the instability aloft. Thunderstorms were reported producing severe size hail, with temperatures still at 40 degrees at the surface. As temperatures warmed, stronger winds also accompanied the hail, especially along the southern KY border. This resulted in several downed trees across the area. In addition, with such high winds above the stable layer, these storms were very progressive, with storm motions as high as 80 mph to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported hail estimated at 1 inch from a firehouse in the community of Eubank.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rain resulted in some isolated flash flooding in the Purchase area of west Kentucky. As a 500 mb trough ejected into the central U.S, a prefrontal surface trough acted to quickly spread a swath of heavy rain into the region by daybreak. A surface warm front approached the region from the south, adding to rainfall totals during the day. Within a large area of steady rain, isolated elevated thunderstorms occurred north of the warm front. Storm total rainfall was mostly in the 2 to 4 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Highway 60 was flooded at an intersection in Paducah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread freezing rain lead to an ice storm across eastern Kentucky on February 10th and 11th, 2021. Many locations experienced power outages due to ice laden trees and tree limbs falling onto and/or downing power lines. Ice accumulations ranged from as little as a tenth of an inch to as much as half an inch. The hardest hit areas were generally along and north of the Mountain Parkway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy accumulation of ice due to freezing rain brought down trees all over the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initial round of light snow impacted far west Kentucky during the early morning hours on the 17th. After a lull, snow spread northeastward from Tennessee and Arkansas during the late afternoon and early evening hours. The most concentrated area of snow occurred across western Kentucky during the evening and overnight hours. The last snow band pivoted across the region during the morning of the 18th. Snowfall totals ranged from 2 to 4 for this event. The highest totals of 3 to 4 inches were primarily southeast of a line from Paducah to Owensboro. With temperatures well below freezing, snow quickly accumulated on roads and created treacherous travel conditions from the evening of the 17th through the morning of the 18th. The official snowfall total in Paducah was 1.9. February 18th marked the 5th consecutive day with measurable snow (0.1 or greater) in Paducah. This tied the record of 5 days set back on March 7-11, 1960. Following on the heels of a major winter storm on the 15th, a mid level short wave shifted eastward into the Plains as surface low pressure lifted east northeast out of the northern Gulf and into the southeastern part of the country. Plenty of cold air remained in place for the precipitation type to be all snow with this system.

The Green River remained above flood stage as a result of heavy rainfall on the 25th of January. Some low-lying areas in the flood plain were inundated, mostly fields and undeveloped woodland. The river crested on the 29th of January and fell back below flood stage on the 1st of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding continued on the Green River into the morning of February 1.

Plenty of cold air remained in place for the precipitation type to be all snow with this system.

An initial round of light snow impacted far west Kentucky during the early morning hours on the 17th. After a lull, snow spread northeastward from Tennessee and Arkansas during the late afternoon and early evening hours. The most concentrated area of snow occurred across western Kentucky during the evening and overnight hours. The last snow band pivoted across the region during the morning of the 18th. Snowfall totals ranged from 2 to 4 for this event. The highest totals of 3 to 4 inches were primarily southeast of a line from Paducah to Owensboro. With temperatures well below freezing, snow quickly accumulated on roads and created treacherous travel conditions from the evening of the 17th through the morning of the 18th. The official snowfall total in Paducah was 1.9. February 18th marked the 5th consecutive day with measurable snow (0.1 or greater) in Paducah. This tied the record of 5 days set back on March 7-11, 1960. A significant winter/ice storm struck eastern Kentucky from February 14th to 16th 2021. Ice accumulations from freezing rain ranged from 0.25 inches to as much as 0.75 inches by the the time the storm had ended. Sleet accumulations of 0.3 to as much as 2 inches also occurred. The weight of the ice brought down tree limbs, entire trees, power lines, and power poles all across eastern Kentucky. The highest ice accumulations occurred primarily along the higher ridges around eastern Kentucky with much lower amounts in the surrounding valleys. During the height of the icing event, many roads around eastern Kentucky were impassable or nearly so. Many power outages resulted from downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter of an inch of ice accumulation due to freezing rain occurred in and around Oneida.

With a stationary boundary parked just north of the state, and deep S to SW flow, much of the state was seeing ample amounts of warm air and moisture being advected in. Afternoon storms developed each day, each capable of producing torrential rainfall. While the impacts were somewhat isolated on the 9th and 10th (Wednesday and Thursday), more scattered to widespread convection was forecast for Friday the 11th, leading to a Flash Flood Watch being issued through the day. Multiple flash flood reports were received this day, with the hardest hit area being Beattyville and Lee County, where considerable flash flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alsile Road, west of Williamsburg, was reported impassable due to water flowing over it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of the departing Presidents Day winter storm, Arctic air surged south on gusty north winds. Dangerously low wind chills were observed during the early and mid morning hours. Gusty north winds were accompanied by actual temperatures around zero degrees, including a low of 2 below zero at Paducah. Wind chills dropped as low as minus 10 to minus 15 degrees over much of the Purchase area of western Kentucky. This Arctic outbreak was part of a longer duration deep freeze. Arctic air descended into the region on February 7 and didn't let up until the afternoon on February 20. The duration of this cold air outbreak was impressive for this part of the country. Paducah, KY set an all-time record with 9 consecutive days remaining below 30 degrees (previous record was 7 days in 1978). Aided by a fresh deep snowpack, overnight lows plummeted below zero in many locations on the mornings of February 16 and 19. Another impressive feat was the duration of dangerously cold wind chills observed February 14-16. Paducah went 54 straight hours with a wind chill at or below 5 degrees. Wind chills of this magnitude for that duration hadn't been observed since 1985 in Paducah. It was close to breaking the record for longest duration with wind chills at or below 5 degrees, which was 58 hours set in 1962. The average temperature in Paducah between February 13-17 was 12.2 degrees. This is the coldest 5 day period since 1997 for the city. Paducah had 3 record cold high temperatures and 2 record low temperatures during the February 13-17 period.

A strong and occluded low pressure system moved through the state on Thursday, March the 18th. Deep moisture and steep lapse rates set the stage for convection during the afternoon. With freezing levels being very low, these storms were able to create copious amounts of hail in addition to torrential rainfall and excessive lightning. While only a few storms reached severe hail potential, a couple dozen others produced widespread small hail. Meanwhile, many of these storms began to train across the same locations, bringing multiple rounds of torrential rainfall. This then resulted in flash flooding during the late afternoon and evening hours. Light to moderate rainfall continued into the overnight hours, as wrap around moisture from the passing low took hold of eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Middle Creek is overflowing its banks and affecting low lying areas along the creek, flowing overtop of private bridges and driveways and making them impassable.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Laurel County Dispatch reported a water rescue and road closure due to flash flooding along Sally's Branch Road near London.
